data.meaning_mnemonic: This radical looks like a hat with some legs under it. If all you can see is legs, the person wearing the hat must really be <radical>jammed in</radical> there. Have you ever tried to cram yourself into a hat so hard it covers your body? Yeah, didn't think so. But imagine how jammed in to that hat you'd be if you did.

This is the <radical>history</radical> radical. You can see a textbook and a couple crossed swords. Because that's basically how history works — fight battles, record the results, and do it all over again. History!

Kind of a strange <radical>tent</radical>, I'll admit, but one that will protect you from things trying to get in (that's why it has all the spikes). It's the best you can do, considering you're in a cloth tent.

A <radical>clown</radical> face, with the funny hat at the top, the silly eyes in the middle, and the big laughing mouth at the bottom. This thing is pretty cute, unlike a lot of clowns…

You could also see this as the stand radical on top of the mouth radical, since standing on their mouth is the kind of weird trick a clown would do.

This radical looks like a curvy letter B. What does B stand for? <radical>Building</radical>! This radical often shows up in kanji related to gatherings or to places where people come together… like buildings.

This is the <radical>spicy</radical> radical. See how it's wide at the top, with a little stem sticking up, and narrow at the bottom? Just like a chili pepper. It's so spicy it can't even handle its own heat!

This is the <radical>heavy</radical> radical. See the square fist in the center, grasping the handle of a dumbbell that has two heavy plates on the top and bottom? The radical just looks like it would be heavy, you know?

This radical is the same as the kanji. It means <radical>anti</radical>.

Just in case you don't remember this, here's the kanji meaning mnemonic: The <radical>stool</radical> is in the <radical>cliff</radical>. If someone has a stool deep inside a cliff, they are most likely pretty <kanji>anti</kanji> society, hiding away like that.
This radical kind of looks like a <radical>brush</radical>. The top part is a hand holding the brush handle, and the bottom part is the bristly tip.

You're lost, but you've come across a signpost that says "<radical>correct</radical>" in all directions. "Correct," "correct," "correct," it says. No matter which way you go, it's the correct way! What a relief!

You could also use the mnemonic you learned with the kanji: There is a line on the ground so you stop, making a giant sign pop up that says CORRECT! You obeyed the rules of the road and did the correct thing. Good on you.

This radical is the same as the kanji. It means <radical>machine</radical>.

Just in case you don't remember this, here's the kanji meaning mnemonic: You have a <radical>private</radical> <radical>mouth</radical> that's a <kanji>machine</kanji>. But you have to keep your machine mouth private because it would scare all the non-machine people around you.

See all the arms sticking out of this radical? Those are for hanging lots of coats and things on, because this is a <radical>coat rack</radical>. Kind of a weird coat rack, but definitely a coat rack.

This radical looks like a <radical>helicopter</radical> coming straight at you, doesn't it? You can't see the tail part because it's behind the bigger front cab of the helicopter. But you can see the front windows and the big spinning blade on top.

A <radical>samurai</radical> has come bearing <radical>forehead</radical>s and <radical>legs</radical> that he's trying to <kanji>sell</kanji>. He thought it was a waste to leave all his slain enemies on the battlefield, so he gathered up their foreheads and legs to sell on the black market for some extra cash. Hey, no judging! A samurai's gotta sell what a samurai's gotta sell, okay?
data.meaning_hint: Imagine running into this samurai salesman as you walk through a sketchy part of town. He cautiously glances over his shoulder before opening his trench coat to reveal to you… all the foreheads and legs he's trying to sell.
data.reading_mnemonic: If you run into this samurai who <kanji>sell</kanji>s severed foreheads and legs, you should just say "<reading>bye</reading> (ばい)!" and get the heck out of there. Actually, if anyone tries to sell you severed body parts — samurai or not — you should probably say bye over and over until you can get away from them.
data.reading_hint: Imagine loudly saying "bye" over and over at this shady samurai. He keeps trying to tell you about how fresh and high-quality all his foreheads and legs are, but you just loudly repeat "bye" as you back away from him.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You find a <radical>cross</radical> in the <radical>mouth</radical> of <radical>Wolverine</radical>. What a strange <kanji>occurrence</kanji>. I guess he put it there, but why would he do such a <kanji>thing</kanji>? You ask him, but he says it's a personal <kanji>matter</kanji> of utmost secrecy.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine his muffled words as he tries to explain this with the cross in his mouth. Hm, better not pry further. Maybe he's trying to… X-orcize his demons…
data.reading_mnemonic: This <kanji>occurrence</kanji> doesn't impress <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ), who appears and gently removes the cross from Wolverine's mouth.
data.reading_hint: Imagine Jesus gently taking the cross out of Wolverine's mouth. What a swell dude.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A good <radical>leader</radical> can put the <radical>ground</radical>'s <radical>history</radical> to great <kanji>use</kanji>. You are a good leader. You have studied the ground and dirt of the earth your entire life. You know the history of the ground and you know how to use it to lead your people.
data.meaning_hint: Smell the ground. Waft the earthy smell of history into your leader-like nostrils. What does it smell like? It smells useful. And also like ground.
data.reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>use</kanji> the ground to raise <reading>shee</reading>p (し). When you were down there sniffing around you realized that your people will flourish best if you use this land for grazing and raising sheep.
data.reading_hint: When you use the ground for sheep like this, your people rejoice. You're the best leader ever! You always use everything to your advantage!

data.meaning_mnemonic: There's an <radical>eye</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>, among some <radical>fins</radical>. What happened? You killed a fish, and now you must hide the murder <kanji>tool</kanji>. Quick, hide the tool before someone finds out it was you.
data.meaning_hint: You feel the panic upon realizing what you've just done. This is murder in the first degree. You used a terrible tool to do it, and now you must hide it.
data.reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>tool</kanji> is dripping with <reading>goo</reading> (ぐ). Goopy, incriminating goo. It's the guts of the fish you just killed and it's all over your hands too. Yuck.
data.reading_hint: That goo is really slimy. It's disgusting. You feel sick to your stomach. Please don't throw up, there's already enough junk on the ground with the eye and fins and all.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Someone throws a <radical>cleat</radical> at your <radical>forehead</radical> while you sit on a <radical>stool</radical>. You must <kanji>accept</kanji> this. Then another guy cleats you in the forehead, but you remain on the stool and <kanji>receive</kanji> it without complaint. This is some kind of acceptance ritual for the most prestigious Spanish soccer club in the world. If a cleat to the forehead doesn't knock you off the stool, you'll receive an offer to join.
data.meaning_hint: You're dealing with a lot of emotions right now. You feel the pain from being cleated in the forehead, but you're also glad to have finally been accepted into the soccer club. Suppress your pain and receive this honor.
data.reading_mnemonic: After they <kanji>accept</kanji> you, they give you a small <reading>ju</reading>ice (じゅ) box. It's to help you recover from the trauma of getting kicked. And you <kanji>receive</kanji> the juice with grace.
data.reading_hint: Did you ever play soccer as a kid? Do you remember accepting a small juice box into your little kid hands during halftime? Apparently pros drink the same ones.

data.meaning_mnemonic: When everyone has <radical>grain</radical> in their <radical>mouth</radical>, you will have <kanji>peace</kanji>. That is the <kanji>Japanese style</kanji> of doing things.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine Japanese people with grain in their mouth. The whole country is at peace.
data.reading_mnemonic: <kanji>Peace</kanji> is the <kanji>Japanese style</kanji>. The <reading>wa</reading>lruses (わ) living off of Japan see this, and start to emulate.
data.reading_hint: The walruses also achieve peace by putting grain in every walrus' mouth, following the Japanese style.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You build a <radical>roof</radical> over your <radical>coat rack</radical> because you <kanji>determine</kanji> that this is the best way to protect your coats from the elements.
data.meaning_hint: Think back on all the experiments and calculations you did to determine this. You tested all kinds of things — umbrellas, awnings, tarps — but ultimately you determined that only a roof would suffice.
data.reading_mnemonic: Why did you <kanji>determine</kanji> your coat rack needed a roof? Because your coat pockets are full of <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots! You can't afford to let your tater tots get rained on, so you determined they needed to be properly sheltered with a roof.
data.reading_hint: Stick your hands into your coat pockets and feel those crispy, toasty taters. You're not gonna let them come to any harm.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Climb onto the <radical>roof</radical> in <radical>spring</radical> to see the <kanji>truth</kanji>. The world is waking back up from winter and revealing the <kanji>reality</kanji> of what it is all around you.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine sitting comfortably on your roof as the world around you changes to spring. Snow melts and reveals all of the truths that have been obscured and hidden in winter.
data.reading_mnemonic: At the moment, you're trying to learn the <kanji>truth</kanji> about the deer that is coming out of the snowy woods when you see not one, but two baby deer appear with it! <reading>Gee! Two</reading> (じつ) babies! You keep looking around and realize that all of the animals seem to have two babies that they had hidden in the snow.
data.reading_hint: Gee! Two babies! Reality sure is lovely.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Take the <radical>moon</radical> <radical>stamp</radical> on the <radical>stool</radical> in front of you and use it on all of your <kanji>clothes</kanji>. Now even if you sell or lose a piece of <kanji>clothing</kanji>, you'll be able to find it again by checking for your special moon stamp.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine placing each piece of clothing you own onto the stool and carefully stamping them with a symbol of the moon. This should stop people from stealing all of your clothes!
data.reading_mnemonic: A few days later you're with a friend when you recognize the <kanji>clothes</kanji> they're wearing. You grab it, rip it off, and scream, "<reading>F**k</reading> (ふく) you!" There is your moon stamp! These are your clothes!!
data.reading_hint: Your friend turns red and tries to pull the clothes back, "F**k you!" they yell. "No, f**k you, these are my clothes and I have proof this time!"

data.meaning_mnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> is going to last an <radical>eternity</radical> you better learn how to <kanji>swim</kanji> now. We'll be under water for the rest of time and if you can't swim you're not gonna make it.
data.meaning_hint: Picture most of the earth's population already swimming on the tsunami that will last the rest of eternity. Your floaties and life preservers would help you through this mess. You need to swim!
data.reading_mnemonic: <reading>Oy</reading> (およ)! Oy, stop daydreaming about <kanji>swim</kanji>ming and actually do it!
data.reading_hint: Picture yourself swimming in the tsunami with everyone else. You can't really talk to them, you're too busy swimming for your life, so you just shout "Oy!" to them and they shout it back.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>cow</radical> with <radical>wing</radical>s is flying in the air. What a strange <kanji>thing</kanji>! You've heard of flying pigs before, but cows? This is a new and strange thing indeed! Cows with wings! What a silly thing.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine if cows could fly. Wouldn't you be jealous of them? I mean, if a cow can fly, any old thing should be able to, right?
data.reading_mnemonic: You want a <kanji>thing</kanji> to help you fly with the cow so you put on your flying <reading>boots</reading> (ぶつ). Your flying boots are a pretty strange thing in themselves, but they're the only way you can keep up.
data.reading_hint: Imagine a thing like flying boots. Do you believe such a thing could be real?

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>flowers</radical> have grown <radical>old</radical>, so they're <kanji>suffering</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Flowers, like anything that grows old, go through a lot of suffering as their bodies break down. They're no different from you and me.
data.reading_mnemonic: If a flower or a person is growing old and <kanji>suffering</kanji>, give them a <reading>coo</reading>kie (く).
data.reading_hint: No one can resist a good cookie. It will ease your suffering, or at least distract you for a little bit.

data.meaning_mnemonic: If your <radical>rib cage</radical> is suddenly <radical>kick</radical>ed you'll probably want to <kanji>express</kanji> something. Anger or surprise, I'm guessing. At any rate a lot will get expressed on the <kanji>surface</kanji> of your face.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine taking a kick to the rib cage out of nowhere. Ow. Okay, now how would you express yourself to your attacker? Maybe show it on the surface, wear your heart on your sleeve? Or pretend it didn't bother you?
data.reading_mnemonic: The only thing you can think to <kanji>express</kanji> is "<reading>Heyo</reading>" (ひょう)! Kind of a cross between "hey" and "yo." Like "heyo, stop it!" It doesn't really match the moment, but it's how you expressed yourself.
data.reading_hint: Imagine yourself saying it more like ひょう, though. ひょう, stop it! Hm… Maybe you should express yourself through retributive violence or something instead.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>clown</radical> <radical>building</radical> is over in that <kanji>part</kanji> of town. We keep the clown part as separate as we can from the rest of the normal people. In fact, the entire clown education <kanji>department</kanji> and their clown <kanji>club</kanji>s have to stay in their part, they can't come in ours.
data.meaning_hint: Of course, we feel terrible about forcing the clowns into their own part of town. It's not that they're lesser than us, it's just that we don't like them.
data.reading_mnemonic: You see, that <kanji>part</kanji> of town is full of <reading>boo</reading>ze (ぶ). Clowns need a lot of booze to keep their charade going, so we just let them have it. But they aren't allowed in our part anymore, because they all stink of nasty clown booze.
data.reading_hint: Picture all of the clowns, trapped in their weird clown part of town, drinking booze and smashing each other over the heads with the bottles. You're glad you don't live in that part.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You put a grain in your mouth and let it take a ride. This kanji has the same radicals as 和, but this time the grain is in your mouth, which is why there's little pieces of grain sticking out of the 口 part ;)
data.meaning_hint: Just imagine you're a car or a train and the grain is a person. It's going for a nice ride in your mouth!
data.reading_mnemonic: You decide the grain is probably lonely on this <kanji>ride</kanji> alone, so you add some <reading>no</reading>ri (の) seaweed, so it has a friend.
data.reading_hint: Now the nori is going for a ride too, woohoo!

data.meaning_mnemonic: Under your <radical>roof</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> there are more <radical>mouth</radical>s to feed because you let <kanji>guest</kanji>s stay with you when it's cold. Some of them aren't family, so they have to pay to stay under your roof and get food. Those are <kanji>customer</kanji>s.
data.meaning_hint: Picture all of the hungry guests and customers under your roof, eating your food and sleeping on your floor.
data.reading_mnemonic: One of your <kanji>guest</kanji>s is a regular named <reading>Kyaku</reading> (きゃく) the <kanji>customer</kanji>. Kyaku is always staying over at one place or another, so everyone knows their name.
data.reading_hint: Kyaku looks just like any other customer. Picture a stereotypical "customer" type person in your head.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You plant a <radical>flag</radical> in the <radical>ground</radical>, marking your <radical>private</radical> patch of <radical>dirt</radical>. In this spot you raise a <kanji>roof</kanji>, then fill the space beneath it with some <kanji>shop</kanji>s and <kanji>store</kanji>s.
data.meaning_hint: That's the natural next progression of claiming some land. Building a roof upon it and building some shops and stores inside. You'll see why in a moment.
data.reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>roof</kanji> covering the dirt is there so that the <reading>Ya</reading>nkees (や) can play baseball year round. The <kanji>store</kanji>s and <kanji>shop</kanji>s are there to sell hot dogs and Yankees memorabilia.
data.reading_hint: You have yourself a whole Yankees stadium, and all it took was a roof and some shops and stores! If you build it, they will come.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>yurt</radical>'s <radical>stool</radical> is always placed at certain <kanji>degree</kanji>s. The degrees change depending on specific <kanji>occurrence</kanji>s.
data.meaning_hint: Since a yurt is round, the stool has to be angled at certain degrees. It's part of yurt culture. Imagine someone adjusting the stool with a measurement tool to adjust it to an exact degree.
data.reading_mnemonic: Also angled at exact <kanji>degree</kanji>s is the <reading>do</reading>g (ど).
data.reading_hint: The dog changes its degrees at every occurrence of the stool changing. Picture the dog leaning at the exact degree the stool is at.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You need to get your <radical>fingers</radical> around a <radical>temple</radical> if you want to <kanji>hold</kanji> it. Is the temple too big? Hold smaller temples you can buy in the temple's souvenir shop.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine holding a tiny temple in your fingers. Hold it up to the light. Hold it on the train. Hold it while you sleep.
data.reading_mnemonic: As you <kanji>hold</kanji> the temple you hear a voice. You look down through your fingers and see a tiny <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). He's smiling and gives you a thumbs up. Aww, thanks Jesus!
data.reading_hint: Jesus likes when you hold things like temples. He probably likes when you hold other things too, like ice cream, puppies, and homeless peoples' hands.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Every <radical>rice paddy</radical> sits <radical>jammed in</radical> to the <kanji>world</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: The world is basically just a bunch of rice paddies smooshed together, if you think about it.
data.reading_mnemonic: To get around this <kanji>world</kanji> of rice paddies, you need to use a <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい).
data.reading_hint: A rice paddy is mostly made up of water, just like our world, and you don't want to have wet feet. Use the power of your kayak to become a world traveler.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Go to the <radical>tent</radical> and take the <radical>lantern</radical> from it, then we will be ready for <kanji>departure</kanji>. We can't leave the lantern inside of the tent; that's a fire hazard. We'd best take it with us before the departure. Don't you agree?
data.meaning_hint: Feel the heat from the lantern. It's not quite as comforting as being inside a tent, but it's better than nothing. The lantern will light your way on your departure.
data.reading_mnemonic: Oh! Don't forget your <reading>hats</reading> (はつ) either. Wouldn’t want to start a <kanji>departure</kanji> without hats or a lantern. That'd be a short journey.
data.reading_hint: How cold and unprotected would your head be without a hat? Very. Taking hats along with departures is integral to their success. Imagine how unsafe you would feel with no hats. That's no departure I want to set out on.

data.meaning_mnemonic: You need to look a <radical>tree</radical> in the <radical>eye</radical> to gain a <kanji>mutual</kanji> respect for each other. Stand up straight, look the tree directly in its eye(s). If it looks right back, you know things are mutual.
data.meaning_hint: It's the same with humans, you know? If you want to have a mutual relationship you need to be able to look each other in the eyes. If you're too embarrassed, or you look down on them, then that isn't mutual respect!
data.reading_mnemonic: If your respect for the tree is <kanji>mutual</kanji> with the respect the tree feels for you, you'll be able to see the tree's <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Right now its soul looks pretty sad, like it's trying to say, "Why are your people cutting down all my friends? I thought we had a mutual understanding."
data.reading_hint: This is basically a FernGully moment, you know? You can feel the tree's pain like it's your pain. It's that kind of mutual sharing of souls you need.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>eye</radical> of a <radical>lion</radical>, still <radical>small</radical> and curious, gazes upon the <kanji>prefecture</kanji> that will one day become his. He's a small lion, but once he grows up, everything reflected in his eye will be his prefecture to rule over.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine the small lion casting his eye across his future prefecture. His small eye is twinkling, because he's proud that he'll one day be prefect, and this prefecture will be his.
data.reading_mnemonic: This small lion's <kanji>prefecture</kanji> is being protected by <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai, because the lion is too small to protect all of this land himself. Ken the samurai is used to wandering far and wide, so one prefecture is nothing to him.
data.reading_hint: Picture Ken protecting the prefecture, punching hyenas in the face, and yelling, "This may just be a prefecture, not a country, but I'll give it my all for my lion prefect-to-be!"

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>sheep</radical> that's <radical>big</radical> is a true <kanji>beauty</kanji>. Big is <kanji>beautiful</kanji>, and that's all the more true for sheep.
data.meaning_hint:
Imagine a great big beautiful sheep. See the sheep's beauty.

This sheep is so big and fluffy that you can't see its tail, which is why it looks bit different in this kanji.
data.reading_mnemonic: The big sheep's <kanji>beauty</kanji> has attracted a swarm of <reading>bee</reading>s (び). The sheep is so beautiful that the bees have mistaken it for a flower.
data.reading_hint: The bees are swarming around the sheep, marvelling at its beauty and trying to figure out how to get nectar from it.

data.meaning_mnemonic: When you're in <radical>prison</radical>, whoever ends up with the <radical>shellfish</radical> will <kanji>lose</kanji>. It's the game all prisoners play. It's like keep away from the shellfish. And if you end up with the shellfish BZZZZTT that's it, you're the loser.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine that it's your first day in prison. You're walking around the yard, minding your own business, when suddenly a shellfish smacks you right in the head and falls into your open hands. "HAHA YOU LOSE!" someone shouts.
data.reading_mnemonic: If you <kanji>lose</kanji>, you're a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). All you had to do was not hold the prison shellfish. It really shouldn't have been that hard to not lose, ya fool.
data.reading_hint: Now you have to try to make some other fool lose. Otherwise you'll be the loser fool forever.

data.meaning_mnemonic: On its <radical>scooter</radical>, a creature with <radical>horns</radical> from <radical>heaven</radical> will take things you want to <kanji>send</kanji> there.
data.meaning_hint: Picture the horned thing on its scooter, a big messenger bag over its shoulder with the kanji for heaven on it. "You got somethin' you want me to send?" it mutters.
data.reading_mnemonic: You want to <kanji>send</kanji> your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) to heaven. I mean, you don't want it to go to some of those other places you've heard of, so why not send your soul?
data.reading_hint: You put your soul into an envelope, stick a stamp on it, and hand it off to be sent to heaven. Bye-bye! I hope this doesn't have any negative effects on your body!

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>heavy</radical> radical and the <kanji>heavy</kanji> kanji are the same.
data.meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
data.reading_mnemonic: In your hand is a very <kanji>heavy</kanji> <reading>jew</reading>el (じゅう). Real jewels are always heavier than they look.
data.reading_hint: Imagine holding a real jewel in your right hand and a fake jewel in your left. The one in your right hand is suuuuupeerrr heavy!

data.meaning_mnemonic: You  <radical>say</radical> things around a  <radical>fire</radical>. In fact there are two  <radical>fire</radical>s, and you're <kanji>talk</kanji>ing with a group of people, all sitting around two nice campfires.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself next to the two campfires, having a heart-to-heart talk. What are you saying?
data.reading_mnemonic: You <kanji>talk</kanji> in this intense, fiery way all the way until <reading>dawn</reading> (だん), but no longer! Once dawn comes the discussion needs to be concluded.
data.reading_hint: If you try to talk all night and past dawn, you'll burn out. Take the day, think, then tonight you can try to have an all-night talk again.

data.meaning_mnemonic: When a <radical>helicopter</radical> is piloted by a <radical>woman</radical>, it stirs an insatiable <kanji>need</kanji> in her. She doesn't know what it is. But she needs it. And that's why she's flying around looking for it!
data.meaning_hint: Picture the woman in the helicopter needing something. She's looking around. Searching. But she doesn't know what it is!
data.reading_mnemonic: Finally she knows what she <kanji>need</kanji>s! It's <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう)! Delicious, creamy yogurt!
data.reading_hint: What does she need? Yogurt!

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>moon</radical> grants a <radical>gladiator</radical> its <radical>power</radical> so he will <kanji>win</kanji>! <kanji>Victory</kanji> will be his!
data.meaning_hint: Watch as the moon's power fills the gladiator. He starts to glow, grows larger, and has some craters on his face now. Well.. okay.
data.reading_mnemonic: He <kanji>win</kanji>s over and over again, and soon the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) is even rooting for him. In fact, the Shougun claims him as his personal gladiator of choice.
data.reading_hint: The Shougun is only interested in people who win. But you probably could have guessed that already.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> is very <radical>anti</radical>-everything, making it all <kanji>temporary</kanji>. You get a new shirt, "I'm anti shirts!" she yells, and out it goes.
data.meaning_hint: She tires of things so quickly, you hardly have time to get used to them before they're gone.
data.reading_mnemonic: The leader's most <kanji>temporary</kanji> possession is her <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Or... cars, because they're all very temporary. She buys one, drives it around, and then suddenly she's anti red car. She needs a blue one!
data.reading_hint: Picture all of the temporary cars she's tried filling up an entire junkyard. They're all a little different, but in the end they were all just temporary fads.

data.meaning_mnemonic: What does your <radical>mama</radical> tell you to do in the <radical>street</radical>? Look before you cross! You gotta do it <kanji>beforehand</kanji>, or it could be too late.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself about to cross the street, and your mama saying in your ear: "always look beforehand"!
data.reading_mnemonic: As you look <kanji>beforehand</kanji>, you see a <reading>yo-</reading>yo (よ) lying in the street.

For example, you can use 苦しい when it's hard to breathe, when you feel emotional pain, or even when your finances are <vocabulary>strained</vocabulary>.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <kanji>temporary</kanji> <kanji>name</kanji>s. These are the <vocabulary>kana</vocabulary> (that's hiragana and katakana). Why are kana the "temporary names" though? The reading is so tricky for <vocabulary>kana</vocabulary> that it makes you say <reading>かな</reading>… (I wonder…) whenever you try to read it. You keep thinking, "Is the reading kana, かな? Or could it be something else, かな?" You're just not sure.

仮定 refers to making tentative assumptions about a specific situation to discuss what might happen under certain conditions, such as "if we <vocabulary>assume</vocabulary> I dropped my wallet here yesterday...”

data.meaning_mnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means both music and bend. For this word, we're looking at "bend." So the verb 曲がる is to be bent. It's intransitive, so don't give it a direct object. Use it to say something gets bent or bends: a knee is bent, the road bends — that sort of thing. Verbs ending in okurigana that rhyme with ある tend to be intransitive like this. You can remember this because if something is ある, it's just there, existing, not acting on anything else. That's why verbs that sound like it are often intransitive too!
data.reading_mnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You already learned this reading with 曲げる, so you should be good to go!

data.meaning_mnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb.

The kanji itself means <kanji>lack</kanji>, and this verb has a few meanings related to that. You'll often see it meaning <vocabulary>to get chipped</vocabulary>, like when talking about a teacup or your teeth. I guess a chipped teacup is lacking... a piece of itself?

欠ける can also literally mean <vocabulary>to be lacking</vocabulary>, as in "a good goalie is lacking from their team." You can use it like this when you're short of something, or when something is missing that would complete a set. In these situations, you'll usually see it as 欠けている!

data.meaning_mnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. You already learned that 通る means to pass, but this is a little different. You aren't the one passing, Sue (す) is. So you have to let Sue pass. In other words, this verb is transitive, meaning it has an object. That's why it means <vocabulary>to let something pass</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to put something through</vocabulary>.

The way this verb is translated into English changes a lot depending on the noun it's paired with. For example 火を通す is literally "to let the fire pass through", and means "to heat through". 針に糸を通す is literally "to put thread through a needle", so means "to thread a needle".

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You know that 受ける means "to receive something." Combine it with 取る (to take something), and 受け取る… still means <vocabulary>to receive something</vocabulary>.

However, while 受ける generally implies passive reception, 受け取る carries a stronger connotation of actively taking possession of something. It is used when receiving a package or gift, or even when interpreting someone's words as having a specific meaning, such as an insult or compliment.
data.reading_mnemonic: If you know how to read 受ける and 取る, you should be good to go.

context_sentences.en: Is there a way to receive a pension from overseas?
context_sentences.ja: 海外から年金を受け取る方法ってありますか？

context_sentences.en: I haven't received my ticket yet, even though I'm leaving tomorrow.
context_sentences.ja: 明日出発なのにまだチケットを受け取ってないんです。

context_sentences.en: Since I'm at home, I'll receive the Amazon package on your behalf.
context_sentences.ja: 家にいるから、アマゾンのパッケージ、代わりに受け取っておくよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: Remember the 〜ヶ月 counter? Replace the 〜 with a <kanji>one</kanji> and this becomes <vocabulary>one month</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: 一 uses the on'yomi you learned with it, but gets shortened to いっ, so watch out for that. The 月 reading is the げつ on'yomi you've learned. You can guess it's げつ here since it's not being used in a specific month's name.

context_sentences.en: It's one more month until Christmas!
context_sentences.ja: クリスマスまであと一ヶ月ですね！

context_sentences.en: I'll be going to school in Japan for one and a half months.
context_sentences.ja: 一ヶ月半、日本で学校に行くんです。

context_sentences.en: I played games at home without going outside for a month.
context_sentences.ja: 一ヶ月間、家から出ずにゲームをした。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Alright, so this one's a bit weird. See that oddly small ヶ there? Well, together with 月 (<kanji>month</kanji>), it becomes a <vocabulary>counter for months</vocabulary>. You'll use this when you want to talk about a specific number of months, so not when you're saying January, February, etc.

Note that you'll run into different variants of this. While 一ヶ月 is the most common, you'll also encounter 一か月, 一カ月, and rarely 一箇月. Luckily, they're all read the same, just written differently. Speaking about reading, let's get to it.
data.reading_mnemonic: The little ヶ might be slightly misleading, as you'll have to read this as か. The rest is easy though, as 月 just takes a normal on'yomi reading. And since we're not talking about specific months, that means it's just げつ, making this 〜かげつ.

context_sentences.en: Japanese schools only have one month of summer vacation.
context_sentences.ja: 日本の学校の夏休みは一ヶ月しかありません。

context_sentences.en: It took several months to complete this.
context_sentences.ja: これが出来上がるまでに数ヶ月かかりました。

context_sentences.en: How many months old is the baby?
context_sentences.ja: 赤ちゃん、今何ヶ月ですか？

context_sentences.en: My child started crawling at eight months and began walking at nine months.
context_sentences.ja: うちの子は八ヶ月でハイハイをして、九ヶ月で歩き出しました。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You've got a <kanji>ten</kanji> <kanji>minute</kanji> <kanji>interval of time</kanji>. That simply makes this <vocabulary>ten minutes</vocabulary>.

Note that, as we mentioned on the page for 〜間, you can omit 間 and this will still mean <vocabulary>ten minutes</vocabulary>! You'll especially see this in conversations and more casual contexts.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which means on'yomi readings from the kanji. You do know all of those, but their behaviour is a little different here. 十 (じゅう) and 分 (～ふん) together actually turn into じゅっぷん here. This is sometimes the case when using ～分 to mean "minute" in combination with certain numbers, so try to pay attention to this!

context_sentences.en: Let's rest here for ten minutes.
context_sentences.ja: ここで十分間休みましょう。

context_sentences.en: Can I play a game just for ten minutes?
context_sentences.ja: 十分間だけゲームしていい？

context_sentences.en: I think I can do it if I have ten minutes.
context_sentences.ja: 十分間あれば出来ると思いますよ。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
If you go far enough <kanji>south</kanji>, every <kanji>country</kanji> is a <vocabulary>tropical country</vocabulary>. Well, relative to Japan anyway… Probably different if you live in the southern hemisphere. 

In Japanese, 南国 often refers to tropical countries or islands like Thailand, Guam, and Hawaii. Sometimes it can refer to the warmer southern regions of Japan as well, like Okinawa.
data.reading_mnemonic:
This is a jukugo word that uses on'yomi readings for 南 (なん) and 国 (こく, but rendaku'd to ごく here). You've seen those readings before, but the なん reading is still pretty new, so here's a mnemonic just in case:

When you're in a <vocabulary>tropical country</vocabulary>, you always get a craving for <reading>naan</reading> (なん) bread. You could be in any <vocabulary>southern country</vocabulary> with a warm, tropical climate — for some reason the heat just makes you want to nosh on some naan.

context_sentences.en: Tropical countries are warm all year round.
context_sentences.ja: 南国は一年中あたたかいです。

context_sentences.en: I was born in a tropical country.
context_sentences.ja: 私は南国で生まれました。

context_sentences.en: Fruits in southern regions are very sweet.
context_sentences.ja: 南国のフルーツはとてもあまいです。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
A <kanji>south</kanji> <kanji>wind</kanji> is… a <vocabulary>south wind</vocabulary>!

南風 is a wind that blows from the south. In Japan it's associated with the warmer temperatures it carries up from the tropics.
data.reading_mnemonic: This word uses the kun'yomi readings for both kanji, which you've seen before. The かぜ reading is the same one you learned with the vocab word 風, so if you can remember that you should have no trouble with this word!

context_sentences.en: A warm southerly wind is blowing.
context_sentences.ja: あたたかい南風がふいています。

context_sentences.en: The flowers are swaying in the south wind.
context_sentences.ja: 南風で花がゆれています。

context_sentences.en: The south wind feels nice today.
context_sentences.ja: 今日は南風が気もちいいです。
